topic: Why people obey authority even when they should not — Milgram

script.title: Why You'd Follow Orders to Hurt Someone

scenes.narration: In 1961, a mild-mannered Yale professor ran an experiment that shattered everything we believed about human decency. What he found still keeps scientists awake at night.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a lone empty chair under a single hanging lightbulb in a dark room, long shadows on bare concrete walls

scenes.narration: Ordinary people — teachers, clerks, fathers — were told to administer electric shocks to a stranger. A man in a white coat said: continue. Most of them did.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white: a white lab coat hanging on a hook, heavy shadow cast across a blank wall, sterile and cold

scenes.narration: The shocks weren't real. But the obedience was. Sixty-five percent delivered what they believed was a potentially lethal four-hundred-and-fifty-volt shock. Strangers. Neighbors. You.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a dial turned to maximum, close-up of a hand hovering over a control panel, deep dramatic shadows

scenes.narration: Milgram called it the agentic state. When you defer to authority, your sense of personal responsibility doesn't disappear — it transfers. You become an instrument, not a person.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white: heavy iron chains wrapped around an empty chair, shadows pooling on the floor, symbolizing invisible psychological restraint

scenes.narration: Participants sweated. They trembled. Some laughed nervously. They knew something felt wrong. They pressed the button anyway. The authority voice said it was necessary.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a single telephone handset lying off the hook on a desk, cord twisted, dim light from a cracked door in background

scenes.narration: This isn't ancient history. Milgram's setup has been replicated across fifty countries and sixty years. The percentage who comply barely moves. The experiment keeps finding the same person — us.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white: rows of identical empty chairs facing a blank wall, overhead fluorescent light casting harsh shadows, eerily uniform

scenes.narration: The real horror isn't that evil people do evil things. It's that good people stop asking whether they should — the moment someone with a title tells them to keep going.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white film noir: a cracked mirror reflecting a fragmented silhouette, sharp contrast between light and dark, identity broken

scenes.narration: Milgram said the most dangerous word in the human language isn't hate. It's 'I was just following orders.' You have heard that sentence before. History is full of it.
scenes.image_prompt: Black and white: a locked door with a heavy bolt, light seeping under the gap, a single set of keys hanging on a nail just out of reach
